title: New Approach to Matrix Perturbation: Beyond the Worst-Case Analysis
speaker: Van H. Vu
field_msc: 15 Linear and multilinear algebra; matrix theory
abstract:
Matrix-perturbation bounds quantify how the spectral characteristics of a baseline matrix A change under additive noise E. Classical results, including Weyl’s inequality for eigenvalues and the Davis–Kahan theorem for eigenvectors and eigenspaces, have long played a foundational role in mathematics. These bounds are known to be sharp in worst-case analyses.
In the past decade, we have developed a perturbation framework that leverages the interaction between E and the eigenvectors of A. This perspective yields quantitative improvements over classical bounds, particularly when E is random, a common scenario in real-world applications.
This talk surveys these developments and main ideas, focusing on recent results concerning eigenspace perturbation.  If time allows, we will discuss extensions to other spectral functionals and applications in different areas.

proof_stages.label: Worst-case perturbation setting and gap obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a symmetric ground-truth matrix A, noisy observation A+E, and spectral functionals of interest, then recalls Weyl- and Davis-Kahan-type operator-norm bounds and explains why eigenvalue gaps enter worst-case eigenspace perturbation.

proof_stages.label: Relative spectral interaction framework
proof_stages.summary: A relative strength of the noise is introduced by testing E against the relevant eigenvectors of A, and this is placed in a general truncated spectral-functional framework F_{f,S}(A), with the talk specializing to f=1 projection perturbations.

proof_stages.label: Low-rank relative eigenspace theorem
proof_stages.summary: For low-rank ground truth, the new deterministic theorem bounds projection error by terms involving the reduced interaction parameters x and y, plus a signal-to-noise term ||E||/lambda_p, under assumptions comparing noise to the signal rather than only to the eigengap.

proof_stages.label: Effective-rank general theorem and sharpness
proof_stages.summary: For full-rank A, a safe spectral distance defines a problem-dependent effective rank, yielding the same x,y form of the bound with lambda_bar and with claimed sharpness of the assumptions and terms, as well as extensions to rectangular matrices, arbitrary index sets, general functions, and eigenvalue perturbations.

proof_stages.label: Application mechanism through structured noise
proof_stages.summary: Because many data matrices are low-rank or effectively low-rank and many noises are random or problem-generated, the theory applies to matrix completion, privacy-preserving low-rank approximation, spiked covariance models, and related spectral algorithms.

proof_stages.label: Contour-resolvent proof scaffold
proof_stages.summary: Cauchy's formula represents truncated spectral functionals as contour integrals of resolvents, and the resolvent identity expands the perturbation into a series whose crude norm estimates recover Davis-Kahan while a bootstrap refinement still leaves a strong gap condition and suboptimal rank dependence.

proof_stages.label: Combinatorial contour expansion
proof_stages.summary: The proof splits each resolvent into important and remainder spectral pieces, expands products into P/Q words, discards the all-remainder word by contour integration, extracts x and y from the first two interaction orders, and groups higher-order terms by graphs to show they are negligible.

examples.name: Two-coordinate adversarial eigenvalue swap
examples.note: A diagonal matrix with nearly tied top eigenvalues is perturbed only in the first two coordinates, causing the top eigenvector to switch and thereby motivating both the gap condition and the later non-adversarial relative-noise viewpoint.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes a concrete diagonal matrix and perturbation, computes the noise norm and changed eigenvalues, and explains the resulting eigenvector swap.

passes.reason: Rank-two diagonal perturbation at a tiny spectral gap swaps the top eigenvector, a worst-case obstruction to gap-free bounds.
A rank-two/sparse diagonal perturbation is a minimal worst-case construction that forces an eigenvalue swap and shows the gap condition is necessary.
a minimal two-coordinate diagonal perturbation at the eigenvalue-crossing threshold shows worst-case eigenspace instability and sharpness of the gap requirement
A rank-two diagonal perturbation collapses the problem to the minimal two-eigenvalue gap case and is used as an adversarial sharp obstruction to gap-free eigenvector bounds.
A two-coordinate diagonal near-crossing with aligned rank-two noise is a minimal tight adversarial obstruction to gap-free eigenvector stability.
a collapsed two-coordinate diagonal perturbation sharply forces an eigenvalue/eigenvector swap, showing gap-free eigenvector control fails.
Used as an adversarial near-crossing obstruction, collapsed to a sharp two-coordinate boundary case.
A minimal two-coordinate diagonal construction at the eigenvalue-swap threshold is used as an adversarial obstruction to any no-gap eigenvector bound.
A two-dimensional diagonal toy perturbation concentrated on the two leading coordinates is an adversarial tight eigenvector-swap obstruction to gap-free bounds.
a minimal two-coordinate diagonal adversarial pair realizes a sharp eigenvector swap showing gap dependence is necessary
an adversarial two-eigenvalue construction at the eigenvalue-crossing boundary shows a small perturbation can swap leading eigenvectors, making the gap dependence necessary.
A two-coordinate diagonal rank-two perturbation is an adversarial minimal construction that realizes a tight eigenvalue/eigenvector swap, obstructing gap-free bounds.

examples.name: Independent-entry and GOE noise cancellation
examples.note: For Gaussian or sub-Gaussian independent-entry noise, the operator norm is of order sqrt(n) while fixed-eigenvector bilinear forms and cross inner products are much smaller, illustrating why the relative parameters x and y improve over worst-case norm bounds.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker estimates the GOE/independent-entry noise sizes, explaining cancellation and deriving orders such as operator norm about square-root n, x order one, and y smaller after normalization.

passes.reason: GOE and i.i.d. white-noise models have high distributional symmetry and illustrate relative-norm cancellation.
Independent-entry noise is used as a standard random model illustrating cancellation, with the GOE case having orthogonal-invariance symmetry.
independent-entry and especially GOE noise are random-noise models used to exhibit cancellation rather than to refute a claim
Independent-entry noise is a standard random-noise model, with the GOE subcase orthogonally invariant, used positively to show cancellation rather than to refute a claim.
GOE/independent-entry noise is a standard random ensemble, with the GOE component orthogonally invariant, used to exhibit bilinear-form cancellation.
independent-entry noise and especially GOE noise are regular random ensembles used to show cancellation rather than failure of a claim.
Independent-entry noise illustrates cancellation, with the GOE case orthogonally invariant rather than adversarial.
Independent-entry/GOE noise is used positively to illustrate cancellation and smaller relative perturbation, with GOE contributing orthogonal-invariance symmetry.
Independent-entry noise and the GOE are used to illustrate random cancellation rather than to refute a claim, with GOE providing an orthogonally invariant Gaussian model.
iid-entry and GOE noise are used as standard random models where cancellations make the relative norms small
independent-entry/GOE noise is used as a positive model where random-direction cancellation makes the relative norm small, with GOE having orthogonal-invariance symmetry.
Independent-entry noise is used as a benign random example of cancellation, with the GOE instance having orthogonal invariance.

examples.name: BBP signal-to-noise transition
examples.note: The BBP phenomenon is invoked to show that when noise intensity exceeds the signal, leading eigenvectors become essentially random, making the theorem's signal-size assumption unavoidable.
examples.engagement_reason: the BBP phenomenon is invoked as a known signal-to-noise threshold where noise overwhelms the signal, without deriving or applying it concretely.

passes.reason: The BBP critical threshold is the boundary where noise overwhelms the spike, obstructing recovery below that SNR.
The BBP transition is a critical signal-to-noise boundary used as an obstruction showing recovery fails below threshold.
the BBP threshold is a critical signal-to-noise boundary showing that below it eigenvectors lose correlation, obstructing stronger perturbation guarantees
The BBP phenomenon is a critical signal-to-noise threshold and is invoked as an obstruction showing that recovery assumptions cannot be substantially weakened.
The BBP threshold is the critical boundary where subthreshold spikes lose eigenvector information, cited as an unavoidable obstruction.
the BBP threshold is a critical boundary where below-threshold signal recovery/eigenvector correlation fails.
The BBP transition is a critical threshold showing recovery cannot hold below the signal-to-noise boundary.
The BBP threshold is a critical signal-to-noise boundary showing that below it eigenvector recovery cannot retain signal information.
The BBP transition is invoked as the critical signal-to-noise threshold obstructing recovery when noise overwhelms the spike.
the BBP threshold is a critical boundary showing that below sufficient signal-to-noise the eigenvector information is lost
the BBP transition is a critical signal-to-noise threshold used as an obstruction showing the signal-strength assumption cannot be removed.
The BBP transition is cited as the critical signal-to-noise threshold showing that below it the spike information is lost.

examples.name: Matrix completion and Netflix ratings
examples.note: A low-rank matrix with randomly missing entries, motivated by the Netflix ratings problem and accompanied by an incoherence obstruction, is rescaled into an unbiased A+E observation to demonstrate an application of the relative perturbation theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: the matrix completion/Netflix setting is formulated in detail, with random observation, rescaling to an unbiased matrix, writing the model as A plus E, and applying the perturbation framework to a spectral approximation algorithm.
